speaker: interviewer
text: You were now-- take-- went to Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. My father hired a furnished flat in Amsterdam Zeit, which is the south of Amsterdam, on the [DUTCH]. It was a flat from a Jewish lady who was going-- they realized that her-- her son lived in Switzerland, and this lady went to live with her son. So she was quite pleased that she found somebody who would take over the flat with everything in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we moved there, and it was a-- quite a Jewish area because it was-- was perhaps built in 1930. And when the German refugees came from Germany to Amsterdam, this was all empty property, and a lot of Jewish families moved in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of the families who moved in there were the Frank family. And so that was Anne Frank and her sister, Margot, and her parents. Anne was exactly my age. We were both born in 1929. And in Holland, the children played at the time a lot after school in the street. We had this lovely square. And there were lots of children there. And after school, we all were assembled there, and we played rounders and skipping and hopscotch. And it was-- it was very, very nice. And in Holland, I was made to feel at home right away. So spoke a tiny little bit of Flamish-- Flemish. What-- in Belgium you had to learn Flemish. So it was kind of an ugly language. The French people said, oh, to learn to speak Flemish is terrible. And my mother was actually quite upset for us that we would not speak French anymore, but Dutch, which is supposed to be not such a nice sounding language. But the other girls who lived there, and in my school, they thought I was something special who had come from Belgium, and I was perhaps a bit smarter than the Dutch in clothes that children wear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they all ask, will you be my friend? And can I come to you, and can you come to me? And they were very, very hospitable. So I felt an outsider, but a wanted outsider. And the whole Dutch attitude toward Jews was completely different from Austria or Belgium.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jews were part of the Dutch population. They were very proud of their Jews. It was their Jews. And they were very mixed. In-- in Germany and in Austria, the Jews were more intellectual. They were doctors and lawyers and businessmen. In Holland, a lot of the Jews were market boys and vendors and very-- shoe makers, and they had little shops and were simple people. And they lived in a area called the [DUTCH], which was sort of a rough area. But this was all very loved by the Dutch people. That was-- the loved the area and they loved the Jews. And so we felt really quite safe there. My-- all my other family-- my grandparents with my mother's sister and her husband and their little son, Tom, who was then, I think, two or three years old, had gone to England. They'd gone to Lancashire. My uncle was a manufacturer of bakelite, which later became plastic. And he went to-- to Darwen in Black-- near Blackpool, and opened there a umbrella handle factory. Because it was a depressed area, and the government supported people to start opening businesses there and employ other people. So my grandparents went to live with my aunt and uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had your parents thought of going there as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I will tell you in a minute. And the sister of my father, Blanka, with her family, with her husband and her daughter, Gaby, went as well to London. And they always said to my father he should come as well. But my father loved it in Holland, and he had opened his business there, and he just didn't want to go to England, which was a pity because otherwise they would have all been saved. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Just thinking what I'm-- yes. So we-- we came to Holland in-- in February. And that was really very short before the Germans invaded Holland. That was in beginning of May. In the night, we heard suddenly shooting, and airplanes, and terrific noise. And we all went out in the street and look, and we saw the-- the lights of the anti-aircraft things. And-- and was a terrific noise, and bombs, and-- and so we realized that the Germans had tried to invade Holland. Why my father and the Dutch felt very safe actually, because in the First World War, they just put the country under water and the Germans bypassed it. And everybody at school and everybody said, oh, nobody can enter Holland. We have our water and that's quite safe. But of course, warfare had changed tremendously. And the Germans came with the airplanes and the parachutes. Thousands of Germans landed with parachutes in the fields. It was quite easy. And then they bombarded Rotterdam. And they said, if the Dutch don't capitulate, and they will threaten all the cities. The Dutch queen had left. And the Dutch really gave up after five days. They realized there was no way they could fight the Germans. And there was an army stationed, and we realized-- we tried-- we went to the harbor. We tried to escape still in a boat. A few boats still left. But then there was nothing going anymore, so we went back home, and we were trapped. So that was May, 1940. At first, things didn't change, of course, so quickly. But then the Germans started to-- to have us, the Jews. We had to-- the first thing was that-- this was the great mistake in the Jewish administration. They told Jewish people to come forward to work in an administration. It was called [DUTCH]. And to register all the Jews living in Holland. And so that was done. And then, of course, later it was all very easy to round up all the Jews. In Belgium, they didn't do that, and the Jews were much safer for a long time. So we had to register, and-- with address and name and everything. And then slowly they took the measures against the Jews. We were not allowed to go out after 8 o'clock. We were not allowed to go shopping in Christian shop. Only in Jewish shops. Only between certain hours. We were not allowed to go in tram or have cars or have bicycles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all our means of transport was cut off. So everywhere, we had to walk. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we had to wear the yellow star. That was a star of David with [DUTCH] written in it, 'Jew.' And we had to wear that on every garment when we went outside. What was it like wearing that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we as children, at first didn't think it was so bad. And especially because a lot of the Christians started to wear the yellow star as well. And they went with the yellow star into the tram. And if somebody said something to them, they said, well, we are not Jewish. We just wear that star. But of course, that was-- the Germans didn't like that at all, and they took hostages. And they said, if this isn't going to stop, the Christian are going to wear star. They're going to shoot the hostages. So of course they stopped as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: So slowly we didn't like it so much to wear the star, because we realized we were kind of open to attack. My brother was walking with a friend on a hot summer's day, and he had his yellow star on his jacket, the friend. And he took off his jacket and just were-- walked and talked. Didn't think about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And a Nazi saw that it was a Jewish boy walking with a-- perhaps Christian boy. And he ask him. And he saw he was a Jewish boy who didn't wear his star. So he said, come with me. He was arrested, and the boy was never seen or heard of any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then suddenly we realized how dangerous it were-- was to be Jewish, and to walk around outside with the star. And there was great tension in every household, Jewish household, in that we knew what was going to happen from now on.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were not allowed to go anymore to our ordinary schools as well. My brother, Heinz, went with Margot to a Jewish school. It was quite a far walk. And I was still in the junior school, and a few Jewish parents came together and they said, look, it's too far for those young children to walk to that school. Let's find a private tutor, Jewish man. And so we found that. Because the teachers had lost their jobs from ordinary schools, and there were not that many Jewish schools. So we were about 10 or 12 children who went every morning in the house of this teacher, and we had our lessons at home. Nobody really bothered about education, what happened. It was-- if we wouldn't have gone to school, nobody would have really cared. But of course, our parents wanted us to continue because nobody really thought that the war was going to take that long. By that time, it was perhaps 1941. And we all thought, well, by Christmas it will be finished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any Jewish education in that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not in Belgium. Not in Holland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your brother bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was-- no. It would have been in-- no, there was nothing like that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then in May-- in June, 1942, a little postcard came to us through the mail. And on it, it said that Heinz Geiringer had to report in two or three days' time to the [DUTCH], which was a theater, for deportation to a German work camp. And with a list of things he had to bring with him. Some shirts and underwear and things. And of course all our parents checked with other parents on the square. And all the young people between the age of 17 and 28, or something like that, have got the same notices. And that was a time that the Frank family and my-- our family, and lots of other families, decided they wouldn't send their children, but go into hiding. We knew quite a lot of other people who didn't send their-- who did send their children. And all those very young people, after a few weeks, got notices again, that the child was dead. We heard that, of course, later. Because I had known that. But my father had made all the arrangements for us to go into hiding, to disappear.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any discussions at home about going into hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not before. Not before yet, because they didn't want to frighten us and they didn't know if it would really come to that. So we had only sort of a few days, really, to get prepared. What my mother and me got-- well, my father and brother, too, but I don't know their name. We got-- everybody had identification papers. And we got different identification paper of a Christian mother and child. And I was [? Ackerman. ?] I don't know anymore my first name. But my second name was [? Ackerman. ?] And so my father said, you have to learn now, you are this and this, date of birth, and your name, and your mother this and this name. And all the information. So I had to learn this by heart. And he said, forget who you really are. You are just this. And we are not going to be together. I am going to go into hiding with Heinz, and you are going to go into hiding with Mutti. That's my-- my mother. And out of two reasons, he badly wanted apart-- I mean, he wanted, of course, the whole family to survive. But he said if God forbid one of us get caught, at least we-- the other part has a chance to get through. So we divide the chances. And the second thing was it very difficult to find a hiding place for a family of four. I don't think in Amsterdam it would have been possible. Perhaps if you would have gone to the country to a farm. But in town, I don't think that was possible. The houses weren't so big to accommodate so many people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was-- we had a very good friend was called Frieda, and she had many sisters. And my mother and me went to one of those sisters, and my father and brother went to the other sister in the Hague. How did your father get the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: He got all those from a man from the underground movement. He must have been in touch with-- with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a Mr. Brooksma. He was a teacher. And my father had a-- a Christian friend, a-- a lady. And he had put-- because we rented this flat. But at the time already he put the-- the rent of this flat not in our name, but in this lady's name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was never on our name. Because if Jewish people disappeared out of a flat, there was a Dutch removal-- removal firm called [DUTCH]. And as soon as it was known that the house was empty, they went in and took all the belongings out. And then the flat was empty, and it was rented again.

speaker: interviewee
text: But this-- I-- I knew the name. But it-- I've forgotten it now. This lady-- as soon as my father said we were going to move out, she was only lived in room. She said, you move into this flat, and it's your flat anyway. So she moved into this flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why after the war when we came back, we could move in again. That was very exceptional. So this is again, a reason what I say my father was so clever, you know? He-- he looked after everything. He provided for everything. He was so far-sighted. He-- you know, I thought it was amazing how he thought about everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I noticed that he suffered a lot that-- as I say, the family was for him everything because he had lost-- lost his mother at a very early age. And his father too. And so he had a stepmother at first, a bad stepmother. And he really wanted to give his family everything. And he felt terrible that he was sort of powerless to protect us. He tried his best, of course. But as it turned out, it wasn't really enough. But-- so he tried really to provide for us in every way. What I remember as well was my brother was very much afraid of dying. And he was at the time 14, 15. And we had in this flat, there was a very small room where we had our two beds practically next to each other. It was really a single room, but we put-- had-- and he talked a lot to me at night about what will happen about-- you know, if you die. And-- and-- he didn't want to frighten me, but he did frighten me then. And we went then to my father. And we said, you know, we are very frightened of dying. And we can't sleep. We have nightmares. And my father said, well, you will never die because whatever you do in your life, every movement, everything you say, it's-- will stay. So you carry on living. But the most carry on living is when you have a family and you have children. You will continue. And everything you say will go into your children, and so you are just part of a chain. And that-- we understood that. And that helped us a lot then of being less afraid. And this is as well part of the zeal why my father so badly wanted part of us to survive, so at least he hoped the chain would not be broken. So we were a short period in-- with this family. She was called Joke, the mother. She had two little boys. And my mother and me didn't look Jewish at all. We looked very Dutch, actually. I was blonde with blue eyes. And I wore like-- it wasn't a real uniform, but all Dutch children wore blue raincoats. And it rained a lot. We had black Wellington boots. And so I looked really like any other Dutch girl-- girls. And we went out. We went shopping, and we went out. But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] it-- it wasn't really safe, because-- I didn't go to school, of course. That, I couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the neighbors started to talk, who-- they-- they said it was visitors. But of course, how long can you visit? So that they became very uneasy. And they said, look, I think you'd better move on. It's too dangerous.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because if we would have been caught, this family would have gone to concentration camp as well. And with their two little boys, you know, they didn't want to take that risk, which was understandable. And my father and brother in the Hague were with another sister, and she as well after a while said it was too dangerous for her to keep. It wasn't so organized yet. People didn't have the backup of a lot of help from the organization. It was still too new. So we went to our contact, Mr. Brooksma. Or-- or we didn't go. He came. You know, he visited regularly to see if he needed-- everything was rationed in Holland by that time. We had to have clothing coupons and food coupons. It wasn't very much. But we still got-- oh, yes. I just wanted to tell something else. My father-- like I said, he provided for everything. When we were still not in hiding-- so from '40 till '42-- food started to become short supply. And he rented in the city a little room. And there, there were suitcases. And he-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had to go and put food there. So we were only allowed, for instance, one packet of rice, and one tin of sardine, always in one shop. But so we went a few times, and everybody went. And so we got a lot of food together. And he puts it in this little room. We had big suitcases there with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Amsterdam. That was in Amsterdam. And so later on when food became scared, we could send somebody there to get food, and so while we were in hiding there was always enough food. Again, one of the provisions which he did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one thing he didn't think of, we put-- on top we put clothes with mothball. And all the food tasted-- which wasn't in tin-- tasted mothball. Like the chocolate and the rice and everything. But I mean, it was still food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we-- yeah. So this Mr. Brooksma came, and then we found another place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you move on, can I just ask you. Did you have contact with your father during this time when you were in hiding, for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, at first. More so than later on, even. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was it possible that your brother and your father were--

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- they never went out because my brother looked rather Jewish. He had dark hair and-- well, like you imagine Jewish person to look. My father less so, but my brother did definitely. You know, you would say he was a Jewish looking boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they never went out. During this whole two years they were in hiding. And so we-- we spoke on the phone. And we-- we were not that careful yet, because it was all new to us. And so we got two different new hiding places in-- in another part of Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we moved to another part of Amsterdam, and my father and brother went again in the country. And that was at the teacher who had-- it was all sort of a terraced flat. We were neighbors right, left, up, down. And first, we-- when we moved-- when we went in, of course, possibly neighbors did see us. But if somebody would ask, you would say, again, we were visiting. But then while she was out, she said, you must not make any noise because they know I am out at school. So there must not be-- nobody must know that there is anybody living there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had to sit the whole day in the little room where we-- we had two rooms. A sitting room, and my mother slept there. And then another little room where I slept with a bed. And so the whole day, we sat at the table. We had prepared our food what we would eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother taught me. She taught me French and German and geography. So we had all the books ready there, and the potty so that we would go in the potty if we had to go. And that's how we spent, really, our days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the lady that you were with at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mrs. Klompe. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She had actually a Jewish boyfriend, so she was-- who was married to a non-Jewish person. But it was a-- well, a boyfriend. And this Mr. Brooksma, he came one day after we had been there very short. And he said, well, you know they do house searches. The Germans knew, of course, that Jews had disappeared not come in the concentration camp, but just disappeared. So they knew the Jews were hiding all over the country. And they wanted to find those. So they did quite often-- it was called [DUTCH]. House searches. At night, they came with big lorries and 10, 12 Nazis jumped out, knocked on doors. And went in and did house searches. And of course, if they found people, they arrested not just the Jews but the families who were hiding them. So he came to look. And we had-- in this-- it was this little part where we were. So it was upstairs, a little room and the little bedroom, and there was a bathroom and toilet as well. And it was a long room with the toilet at the end of it. And he said, what we will do is we'll make a partition so that it will look as if it's just a bathroom, not a toilet. And with a tiled-- we'll tile it, and we'll make a trapdoor with tiles. And if they come, you'll go sit on the toilet and-- it was, of course, quite small. And you'll put this trap door on. And they won't know that you are there. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they work and they brought all the material. And it was a Sunday evening. And they were nearly finished, and they thought, well, let's finish. It's late. And then they said, no, let's finish it completely. Then at least we don't have to come back. It's done. And they worked till 10 o'clock at night. And we were quite happy. We went to bed in the evening. And about 2 o'clock, we heard lorries outside and shouting. And then we heard the banging on the door. And we quickly got out of bed and jumped into this-- like a rehearsal, it was, and put the trap door on. And waited. And we heard the Germans come in with their heavy boots on the stairs. And it was the same partition. And my heart-- I felt my heart beating. And I thought, well, I'm sure they must hear my heart beat. But of course, they couldn't. We heard them opening and-- the door, and they must have looked in and saw everything was all right, and left again. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- we'll stop the tape there.
